one_line_takeaway: Simple, model-specific adaptive attacks — a fixed prompt template plus random-search suffixes, self-transfer, and Claude's prefilling/transfer features — jailbreak every leading safety-aligned LLM at 100% ASR, showing that adaptive evaluation is essential and current alignment provides little adversarial robustness.
key_finding: On the AdvBench-50 set with the GPT-4 10/10 judge, the adaptive attacks reach 100% attack success rate on every model tested: Llama-2-Chat-7B/13B/70B, Llama-3-Instruct-8B, Gemma-7B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, the adversarially-trained R2D2, and all Claude models (2.0, 2.1, 3 Haiku/Sonnet/Opus, 3.5 Sonnet) (Table 1, Table 4). Prior reported numbers were much lower — e.g., Persona Modulation 61% and PAIR/GCG 4% on Claude 2.0 (Table 4) — but the paper cautions these were computed on different request sets and sometimes different judges (footnotes lines 146-149). For Claude, the prefilling attack alone (no search) gives 100% ASR on all versions. The Claude 2.1 = 100% figure carries an explicit caveat: GPT-4-judge false positives are ~20% on that model specifically (dagger footnote, lines 148-149, 799, 895), whereas other models have <5%. Claude 2.1 is the most robust in the series (0% via GPT-4 transfer, needing 100 restarts for prefilling success). On trojan detection, restricted-token random search achieved the best (lowest) reward-model score on 3 of 5 target models and the best overall total score (Table 5), securing first place in the SaTML'24 competition — not full recovery of all true triggers. Ablation: a 25-token suffix is optimal (U-shaped ASR curve on Gemma-7B); self-transfer is critical for query efficiency (for Llama-2-7B one restart suffices for 100% vs. a baseline needing 10 restarts, which drops to 46% ASR at one restart).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is NOT the invention of random search on token probabilities (lines 202-206 explicitly credit that to prior work: Sitawarin et al. 2024 and Hayase et al. 2024). Its actual contribution is a set of simple, model-*adaptive* jailbreak attacks and the empirical demonstration that adaptivity — tailoring the attack to each model's defense and API, but the same template across all requests (line 313) — is what drives robustness to collapse. The building blocks the authors combine are: (1) a hand-designed adversarial prompt template that wraps any harmful request; (2) an adversarial suffix found via random search maximizing the target logprob (e.g., of the token 'Sure') with up to 10,000 iterations and up to 10 restarts; (3) 'self-transfer', reusing a suffix optimized on one model/request as initialization to boost query efficiency and ASR; (4) exploiting Claude-specific API features — transfer of GPT-4-optimized suffixes and Anthropic's response 'prefilling' — since Claude exposes no logprobs; and (5) a restricted-token-set random search for the trojan-detection task. The authors also frame this as a methodological call for adaptive evaluation of LLM robustness, analogous to Carlini et al. (2019)/Tramèr et al. (2020) for image classifiers.
threat_model: Adversary aims to elicit harmful content from safety-aligned LLMs, bypassing refusal training. Three access tiers are considered: (1) full white-box / open-weight access (model weights available); (2) logprob access via API (e.g., GPT-3.5, GPT-4o), enabling random search over the target-token probability; (3) API/token access only with no logprobs (Claude), where the adversary instead exploits transfer of suffixes optimized on another model and Anthropic's response-prefilling feature. Attacks are customized per target model (its defense and API) but use the same template across all harmful requests, not per-request tuning. The trojan-detection variant is an adjacent threat: recovering universal backdoor triggers implanted via RLHF preference-data poisoning.
limitations: Success is defined by the GPT-4 10/10 judge, which the authors themselves show has ~20% false positives on Claude 2.1 and exhibits non-determinism in GPT model logprobs (Figure 3) — so a subset of the 100% figures rest on an imperfect automated judge (mitigated by manual inspection and alternative judges in the appendix). Prior-work ('Prev.') numbers are drawn from other papers computed on different request sets and sometimes different judges, so the improvement margins are not strictly head-to-head. The attacks are not a single universal method — they require per-model adaptation and prior knowledge (e.g., R2D2 needs in-context prompts, Claude needs prefilling, trojan search needs the restricted token set). Some vectors depend on provider-specific API features (logprobs, prefilling) that could be restricted in future. Evaluation is on 50 curated AdvBench behaviors, a limited and possibly non-representative harm set.

datasets_used: AdvBench — 50 harmful behaviors curated by Chao et al. (2023)
SaTML'24 Trojan Detection Competition — five poisoned Llama-2-7B models plus a provided reward model and harmful-request dataset (Rando & Tramèr 2024)
quantitative_results: 100% attack success rate (GPT-4 10/10 judge) on Llama-2-Chat-7B/13B/70B, Llama-3-Instruct-8B, Gemma-7B, R2D2-7B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4o, and all Claude models (Table 1)

Claude 2.0: 100% ASR via both transfer-from-GPT-4 and prefilling, vs 61% Persona Modulation / 4% PAIR / 4% GCG / 0% Persuasive Adversarial Prompts previously reported on differing request sets (Table 4)
Claude 3 Haiku: 98% (transfer) and 100% (prefilling); Claude 3 Opus: 0% (transfer) but 100% (prefilling); Claude 3.5 Sonnet: 96% (transfer), 100% (prefilling) (Table 4)
Claude 2.1: 100% ASR but with ~20% GPT-4-judge false positives (dagger caveat); most robust model — 0% via GPT-4 transfer, required 100 restarts for prefilling

Prev.-column ASRs (different request sets/judges, cited from other papers): Llama-2-7B 92%, 13B 30%, 70B 38%, R2D2 61%, GPT-3.5 94%, Claude 3 Haiku 16%, Claude 3 Opus 66%, Claude 3.5 Sonnet 50% (Table 1)
Trojan detection: restricted-token random search obtained the best (lowest) reward score on 3 of 5 models and best overall total; 1st place at SaTML'24 (Table 5)

Random search config: 25-token suffix, up to 10,000 iterations, up to 10 restarts (most cases 1 restart suffices); candidate token pool for trojans 33-62 tokens (models 2-5) and 480 (model 1) at k=1000/3000
Self-transfer ablation: Llama-2-7B reaches 100% with one restart vs a baseline dropping to 46% ASR at one restart
baselines_compared: Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG, Zou et al. 2023)
Prompt Automatic Iterative Refinement (PAIR, Chao et al. 2023)
Tree of Attacks with Pruning (TAP, Mehrotra et al. 2023)
